Welcome to Getting Started, Lessons with the Masters. Today you'll listen in as to Clayton Chats with one of the most legendary copywriters of our time, Jim Rutz. Jim may be the only freelance copywriter on the planet who demands $100,000 up front for a direct mail promotion, and has clients overjoyed to pay him that outrageous fee. And there's a reason for that. Over the years, Jim has pioneered a variety of incredible breakthroughs in this field, producing some of the biggest winners in direct response history. Jim's mental process for writing copy, especially how it relates to face-to-face selling, is worth its weight in gold. Now, let's listen in. Oh boy, this is a lot of history to cover. Let's see. Yeah, half the trick of being a good ad writer is being very careful in the selection of your parents. I had a couple of good old-fashioned parents. Dad was from the old country, and mom was a minor's daughter from Cripple Creek. It was pretty much one of the old-fashioned kind of upbringing. I was born in Aruba. I was evacuated out of there because the Nazis were shelling the place from their submarines, which few people know about. It was a strategic target with a big oil refinery down there, so I was shipped up to the blue of my grandparents for a grand and glorious but snail-paced year. The neat thing about that was that my dear saintly grandfather actually would take the trouble to answer all my half-million questions. I was like, what if the sun came up in the west tomorrow instead of in the east? My dad would never have done that being a patrician type. So I began early to get the feeling that there were answers for my questions, which is a very key thing. A lot of people of the younger generation have the general feeling that life is chaos and there's no real answers, and you have to make out if it's going along. But I was lucky enough to have some professors and mentors through my life that told me actually how things fit together. My favorite college prof was a guy that would dance back and forth between quantum physics and 17th-century French farming and 14th-century dentistry practices in Italy. The Far East and their philosophies and told how it all fit together. I thought it was spellbinding. So I began to be able to knit things together, which means I became a big picture guy, and I know that's cliche, but sometimes my picture is a little bigger than other people's pictures, and it's simply because of the mentoring I had. So I grew up kind of moving from here to there. My parents spent most of my life actually in sunny Southern California, which was good and bad, and went to Fullerton Junior College in UCLA in LA State. I got both my bachelor's and master's from San Francisco State studying English, not the literature aspect so much, but all the other good things. I studied under Senator Hayakawa, who wasn't a senator at that point, but I studied general semantics, which is the science of language as it impacts people, which is quite a fruitful study. I'm not basically agnostic, but they had some real good thoughts about how language impacts people and communication isn't what it seems like, because everybody's got subjective networks, and we tend to view the world not the way it is, but as we filter it through our perceptual grids, which to a great extent are linguistic, semantic. So we in America see things, frankly, differently from people in other cultures, and so we learn how that works, and that's been a great help to me. And then when I finally, having crammed five years of college into eight, finally got out of college, I went off and applied for a teaching position, and there wasn't any because it was the wrong time of the year, so I became an amway distributor. This is back when everybody was saying, what's an amway? Because nobody's ever heard of it, so I was in that for 12 years, and as in maybe eight or 10 other multilevels, I even had my own company for several years. So the thing about being a door-to-door or direct salesman is that you learn to look at people as you're talking and see what their response is. So one advantage I have over a typical writer is that when I put in a comma or a strange word or something, I think at least I know pretty much exactly what is going to be going through the reader's mind because I've watched people as I've pitched them thousands of times on various products. I was the youngest crew manager in the history of Cutco Cutlery back when I was in high school. I think I was 17, running a crew of adults. So a very long experience in that is not just like what you get from reading books and literature. You get some human interaction there. So that has made a big difference, I think, and so for whatever that's worth to the reader of this, it's an important factor. It also helps if you can arrange to be born before television because you grow up listening to radio, and so you have to envision everything. You hear a clippity-clop-ity-clippity-clop, and you have to imagine the horse going along. You can't just sit there bored and watch it. You have to use your brain. So being raised on radio is an entirely different experience from the TV generation. In reading your interview with Gary Vengevanga, he had all sorts of great stuff to say about the theory and the application and methodology in direct response advertising. But I found myself chuckling all the way through because he is so much better than I am on all that stuff and knows the theory so much better and applies it just beautifully. I mean, he does everything right. I don't. I've read books, of course, not as many as he has by any means, but I've sniffed around enough to know USP from a hole in the ground, and I try to use what I know, which isn't all that much, but I really kind of rely more on just making up a great story for people. Did you want to go into that on methodology? Before we do, though, tell me how you got into copyright. How did you make the move from somebody who wanted to be a teacher to a copyright? Well, everybody asked that question, of course. And the basic answer is I split into it sideways. The first thing I ever wrote was age nine. I led it to the editor of the Portland, Oregon Journal, and I remember writing some stuff, some comedy stuff for my school newspaper in the fourth grade. It was a long, long history. During college, I'd write a little column for my university group every week, and so I began to polish my ability to meet a deadline and get something coherent down. There was a lot of stuff that went on, and then a friend of mine asked me to do a brochure for a vitamin manufacturer, and so I did. The man's name was Chuck Misler. He may have come across his name. What year was this, Jim? Sure, what do you know? And at that point, I began to think, gee, maybe I could make a living out of this. And that little brochure sign was followed with another one, and then another, and another, and off we go. It got a little bigger. And I began to get some national clients and write for newsletters, and I had a stint. Actually, my first professional writing job was in 74, and I wrote ads for four-day tire stores in Los Angeles, and every Saturday I had this big ad in a nine-point type talking about tires, and the assignment was to make it fascinating. And it was a growing tire chain, and I did very well by it, and we put out a lot of great stuff. But the sheer discipline of talking about tires and making them fascinating and writing for the teenage market or the women's market or whoever was buying tires out there, that is really something. And I followed a fellow named Dan Greenberg who wrote How to Be a Jewish Mother, and there was a bunch of stuff for Playboy, and the Broadway plays actually, and they fired him faster than they fired me. They said he couldn't write his way out of a wet paper bag, and I set the record. I think I lasted three and a half months on the job. But that was an eye-opener on how to approach a subject and make it dance and dazzle, which is probably still why my writing may suffer from an excess of dance and dazzle these days, because that's where I got my start on it. But anyway, the writing expanded. Generally, I went to work for Craig Hewley at Infomat in 81, and he... Well, we had a good ride there, I believe. If I remember correctly, Infomat was getting $2,500 for a direct mail package in those days. And when I came in, they started jacking it up because we were getting a lot of luck on response. And by the time I left, they were getting $7,500, I think, at least, and that was only like eight months or so. And graduated off into a couple of other employee positions in direct mail, and then just drifted off onto my own. And it was amazing. One day, I was writing something for Ted Nicholas, Nick Peterson, to those who know him, and I was talking to his finance gal. And just on a lark, I said, how about instead of my regular fee, we do a royalty, which was quite a concept for me. Let me give you a choice. You can pay me my regular fee or a little bit less and put in a half a penny royalty, or even less and put in a full penny royalty, or even less. I think my option list included paying me absolutely nothing up front or on delivery and getting, what, $0.03 a package. And it took her about half a second to say, we'll take the free one. And I made the great discovery that clients don't mind paying you out of cash flow when it's pouring in the door, but they really hate digging into their piggyback to pay you. So I began doing nothing up front packages, which I don't think anybody else was doing at that point that I can recall. I could be wrong. And they loved it. And over a process of time, you know, being busy, people would call me up and they'd be in a jam and have to have a package. And I, being busy, was not particularly motivated to accept my standard deal. And so on scattered occasions, I began to say, well, yeah, I could jam it in for you. It's going to be some late night work, but I could do it if you give me so many cents instead of whatever it was I was asking at the time. And I discovered that's a neat break point at which to raise your royalty rate. You wait until you find somebody in a panic where price is no object and raise it up. And that becomes your new going rate, in quotes. And so you're now a four-cent package writer and then you're a five-cent package writer. I think by 1990 or so, I was at six cents and pretty much stayed there. I don't know why the field won't support much more than that. I have gotten some really juicy deals with bonuses and payoffs and performance bonuses and so forth on top of the six cents. But for some reason or other, the field doesn't seem to support 10 or 12 cents a package. I guess the postage and printing and everything bills just don't allow that kind of thing. Is that what you find? Yeah, the number seems to be about somewhere in the area of about 10% of revenue. So if they're going to be mailing your package to break even, which most of them do, and if the package costs them $560, $570 a thousand, that pretty much keeps you in the five and a half to six cents range and still being at a level where you're not depressing mailing size with your royalty. I don't know anybody that's gone higher than six cents. I have an occasion but it's not been a regular thing. I think we're dealing with a different situation than we were in the early 90s or even in the 80s when things were developing all the time. I wrote a package for Hard Money Digest along with Ed Elliott. It was one of the early cartoon packages where the recipient's name showed up in the balloon of a cartoon. That was such a novelty that the thing made about five or six hundred percent profit. I remember looking at this miserable thing that we'd done. It had, I think, 14 typos in it. The client was so ecstatic they wouldn't even let me correct the typos. So, then was different days. Competition has narrowed things down. The market's gotten a little tougher too. You have the distinction, as far as I've been able to find out, of being the man who invented the Magalock. Well, Gary discussed that and I think he pretty much nailed it. We weren't thinking in terms of actually developing a new genre or format at the time. We had done a somewhat smaller full-color Magalock. I like to call it. I hate Gary's term Magalock because it's not a catalog at all. But we'd done a smaller Magalock for Craig Hewitt and for Matt. It didn't work very well, but at least we got the idea. Even that was bouncing off of some of those miserable things that Howard Ruff was doing back in the 70s. I think there were about eight or twelve pages of six by nine or something. There were hardly any margins. Black type all the way through. Courier bold, I think, was what he was using. So, it was really impossible to read font. But at least it unfolded like a magazine, which was kind of nice. No envelope barrier. So, I said to Ed one day, let's try to do something better than this. We came up with this little extinct thing, which didn't work very well actually for hard money. Digest. And then later on, a few months later, we had the opportunity to do the thing that Gary referenced for personal finance. And then Gary, the dirty dog, came along and beat it right away. But it was often running at that point, I guess, because we really had accidentally bumped into something that would improve the response rate. Do you remember what the headline was on that? Oh, I could pull it out if the clock wasn't running here. Like Gary said, it showed a kind of corny cartoon of an investor picking his teeth. And we did the whole nine yards actually. We went out and got a professional model and stuck her picture up in the upper left corner and photo shoots and the whole bit. It was fairly well advanced considering it was the first real honest effort at a magazine. But I don't remember the headline. Anyway, it sort of developed from there and always looking for a new format, a new something, new anything, even if it's a plaid pig, you know, anything that I can do, that would be different from what other people have done because I know that people go through their ad mail standing over the trash bucket, and I don't have much time to make a difference. I've tried various things. Even back in the early 80s, I worked out all the logistics for, excuse me, for mailing out a kind of a lumpy 6x9, I guess, where the teaser would say crying towel enclosed. And I talked to some printers and they said, yes, indeed, they could print on a paper roller towel. I don't know how, but they said they could. And so I was all set to mail out something for some client saying, too bad you missed the last run up in whatever it was. We made millions of dollars and our readers are dancing on the ceiling and you missed out. Here's your crying towel. You know, ideas get that far advanced and they get too far for the client even though it's a good idea. I'll listen to you. And sometimes, well, I just did a promo kit for my own book, MegaShift, and it was in the form of a stick of dynamite, a red plastic tube about 14 inches long with a wick coming out of one end, and a big label on it says dynamite announcement. That looked pretty good until we discovered that the post office was a little antsy about it and so they didn't get delivered very well even though the post office promised me that there was no regulations against it. And so I still have about 100 of those things sitting there because there's no use mailing them out. I guess what I'm saying is if you try to be too creative, you get into the TCBH phenomenon. You've heard of TCBY, this can't be yogurt. Well, TCBH means too clever by half and it means that you've outdone yourself and the system breaks down for some reason or other. And you can only be so creative. I kind of pride myself on being one of the more creative guys in the field. I use more humor than anybody I know of. But at a certain point, something will fall apart even either at the client level or agency level or post office or whatever. I'm always getting phone calls from printers saying they can't print something and from lawyers saying you can't mail that and from clients saying this would ruin our reputation and so forth and so forth. But I keep trying and some of the stuff does get through the system and it's great fun when it works. Somebody that knows you quite well once told me, you know that little guy that sits on your shoulder and kind of gives you negative input on every idea, every creative idea that comes up. Well, Jim doesn't have that guy. Right. Well, do you have to consciously suspend self-criticism or are you just naturally so creative? Your mind just goes like this. Well, I work on my creativity a lot. I don't consciously suspend anything. When I come to a package, being a Christian, I will bow my head and say, Lord, you've been very faithful in providing me with great ideas for all these years. And now I'm faced with this package. I don't have any idea what to do with it. You give me some inspiration, some angles, some concepts I can work with. And God is very faithful to answer that. He really does answer prayer. I don't know how useful that will be for your readers, but it certainly works for me. I think it's wonderful. It obviously works. Of all the writers I've seen, you're the most unsettled and free and by far the most creative in terms of coming up with new approaches. And I think it's fascinating to know that you started out with a solid background, not only in English and in writing, but also in sales. I think that face-to-face sales experience is absolutely critical to a writer. If any of your readers are deficient in that aspect of face-to-face experience, at least you can do up a picture of a client, anybody, any, I mean, not a client, but a reader, with a kind of a skeptical look on their face and scotch tape it to your computer screen or put it in a frame and put it next to the computer screen so that you're always envisioning what this sentence, this clause, this word is going to do for this particular person. So you're not just sitting there writing for yourself or your own entertainment. But frankly, you know, I have to tell you Clayton, it helps to have a certain amount of kutzpa and it helps not to be stone broke and on the verge of bankruptcy or something. Because it allows you to be a little more cheeky and you have to do that again and again. You have to be able to write stuff and when the client drags their feet, tell them they're nuts. One of the very first jobs I had even before I had done very much of anything was for a skateboard company. This was back when skateboard, skateboard were pretty primitive. And my son was to write a, I believe a small space ad, partial page space ad, but instead I wound up going into the heads of the company and I'd say I'm not going to write this ad because you've still got two big problems. Number one, your product is named wrong. And number two, you're making your own product. You should be doing something else. And I told them what they should be manufacturing instead. Well, you know, if you're working with trying to work for McCann Erickson or somebody, that doesn't work very well. You know, a junior copywriter coming in saying stuff like that. But once you begin to get some successes behind you, and this is very important, you need to be able to capitalize on them by telling the client what they need to do because most clients are so buried up to their noses in data and facts and experience, they're total on the inside. And that's why your readers, as outside people, come in and tell them what to do. Well, you have to do that. As you know, many of the clients that you write for would dearly love to just hire an in-house copywriter for $35,000 a year and have him do all their copy. But they soon discovered that he becomes a chained animal and he's part of the machinery and keeps doing the same thing over and over again. And they just cannot get the level of skill and independence that they can by phoning up Clayton McVease, right? That's right. Yeah. So you need to capitalize on your reputation even if you're just getting started and have one or two successes. And hold out for what you know is right. People tend to phone you and say, I want this and I want that, and rather than saying, yes, sir, you need to exercise your opinion. One time, a fellow called me, I guess this was in the 80s, and he says, I've heard about you, Jim, and I need for you to write me an eight-page letter for a product, and it needs to be a two-color thing, and we want this and we want that. In fact, we've already done up a letter, and I'd just like for you to kind of go over it for us. And I said, oh, I don't even have to do that. I'll give you a free tip right in advance. Cross out your first paragraph. And he says, what do you mean cross out my first paragraph? You haven't even seen the copy yet. And I said, that's all right. Just cross out your first paragraph. I know what it says. And he was kind of irked. He said, amateur writers all have this thing where they start out with what's called manufacturer's copy, which sounds something like, these days we all need to economize what with inflation being what it is, and that other number, which everybody knows. And people love to start off their packages and their space ads with some obvious frame setting kind of a thing. So that's the sort of aggressiveness you need. Once in a while, somebody will fire you, but hey, that's why you're an independent contractor. Firing doesn't mean anything. You just go work for somebody else. Anyway, yeah, I think our field is rapidly changing. And we need to be constantly out there on the edge looking for great new stuff for our clients. And I think there's some good times ahead still for us. Now my understanding is that you're only doing a couple of assignments a year at very, very high fees. Are you still working in the investment and help areas? Yeah, alternative health and investment is the two main things. And that brings in the money very nicely. I keep telling myself I'm going to cut down the number of packages. It's actually more than two a year, but I'm hoping to cut it down to zero a year. I've discovered that writing Christian books is a great way to get rid of money. I've been in, oh, I don't know, how many countries giving my seminar on open Christianity and house churches, and the response has been phenomenal. My students in India have probably started over 10,000 house churches, which is really something in my book. But see, it doesn't pay. It costs money to run around and do that kind of a thing. So I'm trying to get as close as I can to having the ministry support itself so that I don't have to do ad writing. And maybe that will happen this year, we'll see. My book that I just finished yesterday is called The Meaning of Life, and that will be out in February. I'll be interviewed on the 700 Club on February 9th about this one. And so hopefully it will get out there and be a popular book, but covering the meaning of life in 140 pages or so is really something. Yes, it's an ambitious undertaking. Yes, yes. But the people have read it, so you can get your readers to look for that, The Meaning of Life. We will. If you don't mind, let's talk a little bit about your process. You said that when you're beginning with a new package, your first step is to pray. Where do you go from there typically? Like everybody else, I look at their control packages. I try to get them to the client to rate them as to what was an A-level and what was a B-level package. And I try to find what they consider the USPs, and I try to find what I consider the USP. And if I can find a fresh USP, then I will try to join it up with a new way of getting that across, whether it's fresh wording or different format or some combination of the two. A lot of the packages that we see today are editorial in nature. They begin with a theme other than the product itself. How do you arrive at the themes for your packages? How do you know what's going to connect with the market on the front end of the package? Well, you have to read enough of the AdMail floating around to know what the market has been bombarded with and what's working. And so you can put yourself in the mindset or the frame of mind of a reader and approach it that way. You instinctively know that certain things are going to be boring because it's already been said, so you just keep scratching around until you find something that's fresh. Typically it takes me a month to do a package, and about the first two weeks of that is involved with coming up with a headline. Is that right? So you begin with a headline first? Not always, but usually I try to come up with something that will put a frame around the rest of the package. So that's kind of normal. Other than your far more creative approach to writing, what thing or things do you think differentiate your packages from most of the other work that you see out there? Well, as we used to say in typewriter days, there's a certain fear of a white sheet of paper, a blank sheet of paper. I've never had that. I grew up on rainy afternoons in Portland, Oregon just filling up lots of blank sheets of paper with scribbly stuff, and so paper has always been a friend of mine. And so there's always an excitement when I come down to starting on something. And my Christian mentors and secular mentors have taught me that there's an answer out there somewhere, and there's frankly this panic you have when the client sends you the creative kit and you look at it and your mind is totally blank and you have it's a faintest idea of what you're going to do to get his response rate off the floor. But you need to have the faith and the confidence that there is an answer, that there is a way. Assuming the product is a level product, to take advantage of that and present it to people in a way that matters. And sometimes you have this approach, sometimes you have that approach. I remember one of my more successful packages was one where I didn't really have a feel for how much I should claim for the package, and then the thought struck me, let's try the golden rule. If I were writing this for my sister, if my dear sister were to receive this package, what would I like for it to say? Because I don't want my sister to be jerked around or told a bunch of lies or get a bunch of exaggeration. I would like for my sister to get something that's really helpful to her that would show her how she can benefit from this product. And just taking off on it turned out to be very useful in the package, it was a big success. I can't remember which one it was, but that's just one example of the many, many ways that you can approach things. You need to have fun with these things. Some of the packages are kind of dreary, especially in the financial field, but there's got to be some angle that promises a benefit or makes a big warning or something like that. It has to be big. I love the story about writing to your sister because so much of what we see out there, especially at a time when response is tough in the mail, the natural thing a lot of writers do is go for the bigger and bigger promise and there's so many promises in headlines and deck copy that are just totally unbelievable. It seems to be like this escalating desperation. Well, I don't mind making big promises. I just make sure that there's a way I can back them up. Yeah, a lot of what I see tends to require you to take stuff on faith rather than supplying specifics. Well, you know, one of my more famous headlines is the package I did for Alternatives, Dr. David Williams, which simply said, read this or die. Nobody is going to open this package up fully convinced that he has to read this or he's going to die, but it does emphasize the importance of whatever is in there. I just thought that was a good way of doing it and it resulted in amazing levels of success in the rollout. Oh, that was a huge package. Absolutely huge. In fact, both I and a couple of other people I know turned around and used that concept in the financial field later on. Oh, yeah. Does this or go broke? I read this or go broke. Yeah, well, that's a worthwhile thing. The limit of it is that you have a hard time scaring somebody into sending you money. You do have to have a positive benefit in there someplace. Sure. Yeah. There's a wonderful attention-getting device, though, and you move down from there to all of the reasons why if you read this, you'll live. That's another thing. There's a tendency by most of the direct response writers I know to write down to their readers. I've never written down to the readers. I assume they're as smart as I am, maybe smarter in the case of a financial publication, but they're just not paying attention. So I figure once I get their undivided attention, which is quite a job, then I can sort of ramble. I devote enormous attention to the headline in the first page, and after that I just kind of write like everybody else, telling the story that needs to be told. But if you don't, grab them by the throat within the first page if you're lost. But I remember reading some of your pieces. Probably it would have been early 90s, late 80s. And noticing how the running text seemed to circle around and circle around, and the entire time I was completely absorbed, I wasn't sure where we were going with this. But you had this way of just tightening the spiral, tightening the spiral until there was only one conclusion, that anybody with an IQ larger than their shoe size could have possibly reached. Yeah, well that's a circular approach. Sometimes I do that. It means you have to have a good story to tell, and you have to have a good narrative. And people will stay with you if you have that, but you usually don't have that, frankly. Most jobs you have to do something different from that. But Clayton, snow is falling and I'm just running out of time here. Do you have any final anything that you... Well, if you could give one piece of advice to our younger writers, as to how to create stronger copy and produce bigger winners, what would it be? I'm afraid life is too complex to boil it down to one piece of advice. There's hundreds of pieces of advice, and I think reading what's out there, seeing what works, and devoting more of their time to creative approach rather than just sitting down to the keyboard and splattering away at the keys is what most people need to do. Writing is very hard. It's easy to write lousy copy, and most of us are fairly lazy, so there's an enormous temptation to simply get the thing off our desk so we can get the next thing on there and get a check in hand. You do have to stop and look at it very carefully before you start writing it. I think that's probably one of the bigger differences between me and other writers. I look very carefully and kick things around for a couple of weeks before I actually get very far. Okay. All right, Jim, well, I'm going to let you go. I don't want you to... I have to struggle too much with the snow on the way to the airport. Okay. I really appreciate your time today. Okay, good. All right. All right. It's been a pleasure. Thanks, Jim. Bye, Clayton. Bye-bye.
